item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article has been replaced with the following image, which is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0):

Source: pxfuel
License: CC0
Title: N/A
Author: N/A
Release year: N/A

I like this one quite a lot. The timeline involved is very important to understand why this scip was so effective in its spread and usage. That combined with the very Factory-esque 'you get the benefit, now keep using or suffer the consequences' vibe makes this a +1 from me.
Edit: just noticed the +100 reached on this one so congrats!

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at anytime.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Previously in the Wikiwalk, the team established that SCP-1317 was one of the objects moved around by SCP-703 in its attempts to "help" Site-77's personnel. Its likely that following such a breach of containment, some documentation would be altered RE: keeping 1317 away from 703. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

All known instances of SCP-1317 are to be housed in Vault 13 of Site-77's Safe SCP wing. Each unit is to be inspected on a rotational basis to prevent any chemical corrosion of their containers. A small subset of the SCP-1317 substance is to be set aside for allocated testing purposes. In the event of the discovery of any additional instances of SCP-1317, they are to be cataloged and their point of origin sought out.

To this:

All known instances of SCP-1317 are to be housed in Vault 13 of Site-77's Safe SCP wing. Each unit is to be inspected on a rotational basis to prevent any chemical corrosion of their containers. A small subset of the SCP-1317 substance is to be set aside for allocated testing purposes. Following a containment breach of multiple SCP-1317 instances initiated by SCP-703 all testing instances are to remain outside of SCP-703's area of effect. In the event of the discovery of any additional instances of SCP-1317, they are to be cataloged and their point of origin sought out.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. But I don't understand how this serves a useful short term ability/use. Most factory items coax you into continued use through a useful short term effect, and then it screws you over. It's got the screwing over thing down, but not the other! Upvote anyway for sure for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this could sound like a factory item, but it needs that "your friends here at the factory" tone. right now its just makeup that dissolves people to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was due to a wikidot seizure, they lost power to their servers for a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah-ha, important detail I missed there. Now it makes sense again.
Thanks.

PS: Image is broken now. Although that might just be my laptop and/or wikidot having a seizure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The rash only develops if you stop using it. That's why they are withdrawal symptoms, and not symptoms of simply using the makeup.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's 1943 and there's a war on, so your lucky if you can get a luxury item like cosmetics. But I still think anyone would stop using this stuff at week 1, when they start to develop a rash. It's not a big leap of logic to determine that the rash on your face is form that new foundation you've been using. Even the ones who keep going would stop when their hair fell out.

Basically, I don't find this as appaling as it shoudl be because no one is stupid enough to keep using it into the later, truly traumatic stages.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was a corrupt businessman in BTAS that gave the burned actor who became Clayface the test compound. Maybe a precursor to the Factory??

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I said it was embossed on the bottom

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could mention that 'The Factory' is written on the inside of the lid?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We live in a scary world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Welcome to the site, enjoy your stay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is what I initially thought, too, but it went into a completely different "good thing goes bad" direction… and it gets more points for being something mysteriously popping up and potentially affecting lots of people, rather than just having had uniquely affected one guy and then disappearing forever (aside from Clayface and his antics, of course, but then, he wasn't really infectious).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This.
It awfully reminds me of when a guy tried to make "krokodil" - the heroin substitute, a disinformation procedure for a flesh-eating disease. It wasn't bad, but the actual thing, people doing it to themselves of their own will is just so much worse, and as such it was a flop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not going to say don't do it. However, I don't think you can come up with any supernatural or anomalous element that's actually more horrifying than the actual facts of the case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Y'know what.

I'm going to make it factory again. If the father of the factory says "it feels factory" I'm going to go with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was actually thinking this felt like a factory item. *shrug*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Made it "potentially" fatal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Might want to clarify that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you have been applying it for weeks, all up and down your arms, legs, body, and face, you will die. If you put a little on sometimes and dont really use it, you will not die. Its not insta-death for using it at all. That would be silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When SCP-1317 comes into contact with living animal tissue, it will begin to integrate itself into the tissue. Continuous application of SCP-1317 will lead to the affected tissues being completely replaced by SCP-1317. In addition, SCP-1317 will replicate the biological function of all tissues it replaces. If the user does not continuously apply SCP-1317, the affected tissue will lose its cohesion over the next five weeks, which leads to the user experiencing fatal trauma.

Why would it always lead to fatal trauma, regardless of the area the makeup is applied to?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: http://www.museumofquackery.com/devices/radium.htm

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know, I've read all about it. The case is fascinating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would be careful with that. The Radium Girls case is really tragic and firmly rooted in reality.

"An estimated 4,000 workers were hired by corporations in the U.S. and Canada to paint watch faces with radium. They mixed glue, water and radium powder, and then used camel hair brushes to apply the glowing paint onto dials. The then-current rate of pay, for painting 250 dials a day, was about a penny and a half per dial ($0.27 per dial in today's terms). The brushes would lose shape after a few strokes, so the U.S. Radium supervisors encouraged their workers to point the brushes with their lips, or use their tongues to keep them sharp. For fun, the Radium Girls painted their nails, teeth and faces with the deadly paint produced at the factory. Many of the workers became sick. It is unknown how many died from exposure to radiation."

What happened was an awful catastrophe. Although the women eventually got some form of compensation, they were physically, mental and emotionally destroyed by the incident.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're going to make a bunch of girls who died tragically into a fictitious monster of some sort?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oooh I should make a radium girls scp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was a part of a documentary I caught on television a long while back. I cannot even recall the name. However, I do remember a few figures mentioned.

Eben Byers was a golfer whose jaw decayed and eventually fell off due to overexposure to radium. The autopsy revealed holes in his brain and skull.

There were also the Radium Girls who worked in a luminous paint factory. They were told that the paint was harmless and even offered experimental face paints to bring home. Many of them developed tumors, necrosis and other issues as a consequence.

Neither of those are the right time period but you can find advertisements on youtube and the like through a quick Google search. I would also suggest looking at the company Tho-Radia.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Links?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm reminded of a rather unfortunate series of cases back in the 1950s. For a brief period, radioactive materials were used as makeup and face cleansers. They were thought to be healthier because the radioactivity would 'burn away the extra dirt' clogging the pores. The results were… messy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: http://www.mangareader.net/1108/flesh-coloured-horror.html

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: False. This is very useful short-term. If someone has a giant gaping hole in their liver and needs a transplant, then using this is a fantastic solution. It works when it needs to and is taken out of the body surgically before the negative effects can take place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe I said it when this was sandboxed, but this reminds me too much of Clayface's origin story from Batman: TAS.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh god what am I reading. creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of a Junji Ito story

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The cost of attaining perfect beauty.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks…I guess?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still don't like it, being an object what kills ya in anomalous way, but at least it isn't offensively bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, i guess it isnt really factory. I removed the connection.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted.
The factory items, while quite often dangerous, usually serve an useful purpose.
This doesn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know, not all factory items have the logo visible.

Yeah I fixed it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The words "The Factory" are embossed on to the bottom of all instances of SCP-1317.

But it's not on the items shown in the picture.

I actually really like the article, it just feels that the Factory link is entirely tacked on.

*I like it a lot better now.
